text: Typically, a monster is the mechanism through which a space becomes scary. Eraserhead, Skinamarink, and the Backrooms seem to reverse this process. The setting itself becomes so terrifying as to make any monsters within it almost secondary. The setting is no longer just where the scary happens-it is the scary itself. Oddly, Eraserhead reads like a prototype; Skinamarink seems to extend this to the ultimate in atmosphere and space as the terror; and Backrooms extends this into an entire mythos where the space is the core source of horror even with monsters there. I find backrooms to be the liminal space horror of the year thus far. Obviously I'm just a fanatic and I don't actively study film theory but I'm curious as to where my correlation thrives and fall short, and are there any movie suggestions that I can watch to further my understanding?
label: analysis

text:
Whisper of the Heart (1995, dir. Yoshifumi Kondo)

Delightful coming-of-age story. Hayao Miyazaki's script has great commentary on the artistic process, and this is realized beautifully by Kondo's gentle direction, bringing the audience into the world of a girl who's much too active and aware to be written off as a child but is not confident and autonomous enough to be mistaken for a young woman just yet. Shizuku fits the Ghibli heroine mold admirably, complex in her characterization but never just her flaws or idiosyncrasies. She's a fully fleshed-out person, weaving in and out of her social spheres deftly but also finding time to chase a cat just because it's there, or to appreciate the beauty of urban cityscapes that most people would not find aesthetically pleasing. Her most endearing and interesting trait is her creative spark. She loves to write and loves books and music, but for one reason or another, she has no confidence in her ideas or output, even though her friends consistently praise her and perfect strangers play accompaniment for her cover of Take Me Home, Country Roads. Her arc is not one of finding and realizing a dream, but one of learning how hard it is to commit to one and just how self-gratifying it can feel when something meaningful comes from that commitment. It's the one lesson every young artist needs to learn, and if you're emotionally honest and consistent enough, you will improve and make something that will really resonate with someone. I really like how direct this aspect of Whisper of the Heart is. It makes me wish I'd watched it as a teenager. If you are an artist - or not, really - this is a great watch. It's criminal that Kondo died before directing another feature. Miyazaki and Isao Takahata imagined that he would have been their heir to the studio's throne, and after seeing this film, it's easy to see why.
label: analysis

text:
Haven’t seen backrooms yet but I found the decision to shoot obsession Centre framed made sense. I immediately clocked the narrow aspect ratio and I think the framing supports a couple of things that I think the film is trying to achieve narratively. 1. the entire film is from the main characters point of view, so to me it makes sense that we’re always looking straight ahead. 2. the main character is narrow in his own perspective, he only does what feels right to him with little regard for others, especially Nikki, so we are not looking left and right because he doesn’t take other perspectives into account. 3. the narrow aspect ratio gives the film a more claustrophobic feel and there is little room for the characters to move within the frame. 4. the audience is trained to only regard what’s right in the centre, which makes some of the scares, like when Nikki stands in the corner of the bedroom at night or the car scene, more effective.

That said, for other movies it might not be serving the narrative as well to have everything centre framed. I’m spitballing, but proper eye tracing and careful editing probably takes a lot more time and therefore money. Maybe that plays into the fact that framing has become less dynamic?
label: analysis

text: None of those things need the degree of lazy, boring constant center-framing (and shallow depth of field, which is another obnoxious modern trend) that Obsession goes for.

text:
Personally I think your experience is what was intended. I think the music, the editing, and the writing are all constructed not to give us time to settle in and reflect on the narrative. The movie is like a ticking clock; it constantly builds tensions and momentum and doesn't take its foot off the gas until the moment the bomb finally detonates.

To me this is what working on the Manhattan Project must have felt like - it's huge and exciting and there's a ton of people and there is so much energy and urgency that nobody has time to think about what they're doing. We have writings from people who worked on the project, saying they didn't even start thinking about what they'd actually built until after they'd built it. The whole movie is about creation without reflection.

text:
My issues were of form vs content. I'm sure Oppenheimer's story with creating the bomb can be made into intriguing material. But the movie's FORM (that is, cinematography, music, and most of all editing) left no room for any kind of rumination, contemplation, reflection.

It's an assault on the senses. A first half where every second the camera cuts and cuts and cuts, the music is incessant. What characterization there is gets lost in all the noise. I'm also not sure why this movie needed the trademark Nolan non-linear approach (had the same problem with Dunkirk). The second half was a series of conversations on a very black and white subject, with RDJ playing a boring villain, everybody shouting, everything is underlined again and again.

The cinematography gets lauded for some reason, but there is little ingenuity in the filmmaking. As others have said, the bomb sequence ends up being underwhelming, but there's also not much room for interesting compositions when the movie moves as fast as it does. Also not sure why this demanded an IMAX release (again, had the same probem with other Nolan films and it feels like The Odyssey will end up the same). It's just not visually interesting.

text:
To me, the film whilst being a biopic really isn’t. It’s instead a portrait of the men who created the nuclear bomb and how that got turned into the atomic bomb project. All done by highly intelligent people but ones who also were “off” and possibly sociopathic. People who took huge risks that they didn’t know would’ve killed us all, and how that mentality continued through to the Cold War and the MAD doctrine.

Oppenheimer is the person we focus on this since we was such an important figure in that initial development, but this is why the film is doing both the nuclear bomb and the atomic bomb timelines with them interspersed.

This is why it doesn’t really hit as a biopic and why it avoids all of those tropes. Instead it humanises these figures that otherwise we all too easily see as faceless scientists who were just good at doing science, and not heavily flawed people that made the world so much more dangerous.
label: analysis

n my last film an actor complained to me that I kept referring to her character as the main character when the other character had most of the dialogue. This works because cinema is a visual art form. Take any scene in movie history. Take, I dunno, the beach scene in Jaws. Don't change one word of the script but take all of Roy Scheider's close-ups away and give them to Mrs. Kitner. Poof, Mrs. Kitner is now who your audience connects with.

text:
It's a very nebulous thing but my theory is that it's not about characters, it's about relationships. It's about what one character thinks of another, and how they interact, and the emotions they instill in each other, and how that relationship changes each character. The mistake filmmakers make is that it's NOT about the audience connecting - but about the characters in the story connecting. That's what the audience responds to.

One of the reasons we watch movies is to understand human relationships, and we respond warmly when they're well depicted on screen. So we connect to characters who are able to connect with each other, whether it's love or hate or respect or disgust. You can make a great hero character, or a flawed hero character, but the audience won't connect with them in any way unless they have a real relationship with another character in the story. It's why Superman has Lois Lane. Their relationship is what makes the characters relatable and human, not his super powers or the action or his perfect looks.
label: analysis

text: Just finished watching Antonioni’s L’Avventura, which is part of his alienation trilogy and where he did not want us to identify with the characters. He achieves it partly by refusing to give us reaction shots in dialogue – characters often speak with their backs to us, with two characters speaking one of them is completely unreadable to us because we can’t see their face, and sometimes both characters have their backs turned to us. Lots of long takes in impressive landscapes that overwhelm the characters as well– again, often with their backs to us – and added to that they don’t act very rationally, but we don’t know enough about their pasts to have any idea why they are doing what they’re doing. They are ciphers to one another and to us watching it.

text:
In general it was, thought I have to say that streaming at home and watching in a big theater are obviously two different experiences. Again the themes of reveling in minor but beautiful details, taking pride in your work no matter what it is, the comfort of routine, the value of the quiet life out of the rat race, the contentment with not keeping up with the latest technology all stood out. But what I did not expect were a number of themes or details that I had forgotten about.

Somehow I had completely forgotten that in just one line we find out that Hirayama's father was mean or cruel to him in some way, and he refuses to go see him even though his father is now in a state of dementia. Without saying much, the film gives a big hint to a reason for his estrangement from his family.

There were constant shots either straight on or in the background of the Tokyo Skytree, the tall spire of a building. (I didn't know what it was, I just looked it up.) I assumed it must have some significance to the story since it's in so many shots, but I couldn't fathom it. Sign of commercialism? Gaudy building towering over his very peaceful, small life? No idea.

The girl with the sad (or is it wistful?) look on her face who eats her lunch in the same park as Hirayama and looks over at him frequently, but never speaks. Kindred spirit? Also someone not in need of walls or words to connect with someone? or the alternative take on Hirayama's solitary life: loneliness rather than contentment? I have no idea.

text:
Not a lot of upvotes here but I have to agree. I love film for the absorption and magical moments it can provide. And yes on very rare occasions, spirituality can come, but it’s a different experience, with a different precursor.

Spirituality is not sensation. I would say I have experienced both, and I appreciate them both, but think it is a serious misstep to conflate them.
label: reaction

text:
As a Catholic, Scorsese’s Silence (2016) led me into the same experience that I’ve had with my most profound encounters with the sacrament of reconciliation (confession). I’m not sure how well I’d be able to articulate it in a way for somebody nonreligious to understand what I’m talking about, but it shook me in a way I’ve only ever experienced in my deepest prayers.

For a less explicitly religious version, Flow (2024) altered my entire mental state for the rest of the day. I’ve seen silent films before but they had “dialogue” with text on the screen. To see a story told entirely without words transformed me leaving the cinema. I felt more acutely aware of my presence in every moment. I went home and just sat in my backyard for a few hours watching the clouds drift, listening to birds and wind in the trees, that kind of thing. In this overstimulated world I often feel I have to drag myself into that kicking and screaming, but Flow took me there and I didn’t want to leave.
label: analysis

text: I felt the same way as a catholic. In Silence and Stalker, another great film about faith.

text: I suppose the argument is that, even if we grant that the Convention was originally written due to classist snobbery (which is imo potentially a fair point to grant, however see the comment by joey-jo_jo-jr for a rationale beyond mere snobbery), by the time of Nicholson's protest this is completely besides the point. Nicholson must insist that rules are followed per se, or he has already lost. In other words no matter the content of the rules, if he admits that Saito has the power to alter them at will then he is admitting that he is granting Saito absolute authority. Regardless of the actual directions of Saito accenting to his arbitrary will would be a true loss of morale for the soldiers.

It was part of the Geneva Convention that POWs who were officers were exempt from hard labor. Nicholson says so. Sure, you're being "presentist," but remember who made the rules of war, European students of Caesar, Alexander, and Napoleon, not Lenin.

text:
It has been quite some time since I last watched this film.

I don't think we should judge the Colonel's intentions by just referencing the first half of the film. Yes, it was the right thing to do; to believe in, and to follow the Geneva Conventions. But does it matter in the end?

The tragedy of Nicholson's character is entirely lost if we judge him only by his actions from the first half. Nicholson was an exceedingly idealistic nationalist, to the point that he aids his enemy against his own nation! And that end was reached by means which are against his very principles, that we saw in the first half: in desperate need of upholding the reputation of the British Army, he 'asks' his own officers, and even the hospitalized men, to volunteer for manual labour—the very thing he fought against.

That really is the beauty of Nicholson's character, and which, indeed, seems like a recurring theme in David Lean's films (though the only other Lean film I've watched is Lawrence of Arabia (1962), which I love with great intensity). In Lean's films, we see flawed British men, who reach great heights and become 'legends' (with the quotes), are blinded by their own ideals, and thus ensues their tragedy. Nicholson's tragedy lay in the fact that he was too obsessed with being noble, and if you sympathize with his character, you are missing the point, and falling into the same trap!
label: analysis
